lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: You have seen nothing then?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Nor ever heard, nor ever did suspect.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Yes, you have seen Cassio and she together.

lines.speaker: EMILIA

lines.speaker: EMILIA

lines.speaker: OTHELLO

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Never, my lord.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Nor send you out o’ the way?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Never.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To fetch her fan, her gloves, her mask, nor nothing?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Never, my lord.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: I durst, my lord, to wager she is honest,

lines.speaker: EMILIA

lines.speaker: EMILIA

lines.speaker: EMILIA

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: For, if she be not honest, chaste, and true,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: There’s no man happy; the purest of their wives

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Is foul as slander.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Bid her come hither: go.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: She says enough; yet she’s a simple bawd

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That cannot say as much. This is a subtle whore,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: A closet lock and key of villainous secrets

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And yet she’ll kneel and pray; I have seen her do’t.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: My lord, what is your will?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Pray, chuck, come hither.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: What is your pleasure?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Let me see your eyes;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Look in my face.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: What horrible fancy’s this?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To Emilia. Some of your function, mistress;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Leave procreants alone and shut the door;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Cough, or cry “hem,” if any body come:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Your mystery, your mystery: nay, dispatch.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Upon my knees, what doth your speech import?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I understand a fury in your words.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: But not the words.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Why, what art thou?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Your wife, my lord; your true

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: And loyal wife.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Come, swear it, damn thyself

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Lest, being like one of heaven, the devils themselves

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Should fear to seize thee: therefore be double damn’d:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Swear thou art honest.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Heaven doth truly know it.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Heaven truly knows that thou art false as hell.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: To whom, my lord? with whom? how am I false?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O Desdemona! away! away! away!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Alas the heavy day! Why do you weep?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Am I the motive of these tears, my lord?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: If haply you my father do suspect

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: An instrument of this your calling back,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Lay not your blame on me: If you have lost him,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Why, I have lost him too.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Had it pleased heaven

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To try me with affliction; had they rain’d

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: All kinds of sores and shames on my bare head.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Steep’d me in poverty to the very lips,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Given to captivity me and my utmost hopes,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I should have found in some place of my soul

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: A drop of patience: but, alas, to make me

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: A fixed figure for the time of scorn

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To point his slow unmoving finger at!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Yet could I bear that too; well, very well:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: But there, where I have garner’d up my heart,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Where either I must live, or bear no life;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The fountain from the which my current runs,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Or else dries up; to be discarded thence!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Or keep it as a cistern for foul toads

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To knot and gender in! Turn thy complexion there,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Patience, thou young and rose-lipp’d cherubin—

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Ay, there, look grim as hell!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I hope my noble lord esteems me honest.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O, ay; as summer flies are in the shambles,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That quicken even with blowing. O thou weed,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Who art so lovely fair and smell’st so sweet

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That the sense aches at thee, would thou hadst ne’er been born!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Alas, what ignorant sin have I committed?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Was this fair paper, this most goodly book,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Made to write “whore” upon? What committed!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Committed! O thou public commoner!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I should make very forges of my cheeks,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That would to cinders burn up modesty,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Did I but speak thy deeds. What committed!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Heaven stops the nose at it and the moon winks,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The bawdy wind that kisses all it meets

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Is hush’d within the hollow mine of earth,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And will not hear it. What committed!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Impudent strumpet!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: By heaven, you do me wrong.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Are you not a strumpet?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: No, as I am a Christian:

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: If to preserve this vessel for my lord

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: From any other foul unlawful touch

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Be not to be a strumpet, I am none.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: What, not a whore?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: No, as I shall be saved.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Is’t possible?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: O, heaven forgive us!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I cry you mercy, then:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I took you for that cunning whore of Venice

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That married with Othello. Raising his voice.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: You, mistress,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That have the office opposite to Saint Peter,

lines.text: And keep the gate of hell!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Alas, what does this gentleman conceive?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: How do you, madam? how do you, my good lady?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: ’Faith, half asleep.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Good madam, what’s the matter with my lord?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: With who?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Why, with my lord, madam.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: He that is yours, sweet lady.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I have none: do not talk to me, Emilia;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I cannot weep; nor answer have I none,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: But what should go by water. Prithee, tonight

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Lay on my bed my wedding sheets: remember;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: And call thy husband hither.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Here’s a change indeed!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: ’Tis meet I should be used so, very meet.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: How have I been behaved, that he might stick

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: The small’st opinion on my least misuse?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: What is your pleasure, madam?

lines.text: How is’t with you?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I cannot tell. Those that do teach young babes

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Do it with gentle means and easy tasks:

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: He might have chid me so; for, in good faith,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I am a child to chiding.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: What’s the matter, lady?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Alas, Iago, my lord hath so bewhored her.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Thrown such despite and heavy terms upon her,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: As true hearts cannot bear.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Am I that name, Iago?

lines.text: Why did he so?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I do not know; I am sure I am none such.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Do not weep, do not weep. Alas the day!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Hath she forsook so many noble matches,

lines.speaker: EMILIA

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: To be call’d whore? would it not make one weep?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: It is my wretched fortune.

lines.text: Beshrew him for’t!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: How comes this trick upon him?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Nay, heaven doth know.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: I will be hang’d, if some eternal villain,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Some busy and insinuating rogue,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Some cogging, cozening slave, to get some office,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Have not devised this slander; I’ll be hang’d else.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Fie, there is no such man; it is impossible.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: If any such there be, heaven pardon him!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: A halter pardon him! and hell gnaw his bones!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Why should he call her whore? who keeps her company?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: What place? what time? what form? what likelihood?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: The Moor’s abused by some most villainous knave,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Some base notorious knave, some scurvy fellow.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: O heaven, that such companions thou’ldst unfold,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: And put in every honest hand a whip

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: To lash the rascals naked through the world

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Even from the east to the west!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Speak within door.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: O, fie upon them! Some such squire he was

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: That turn’d your wit the seamy side without,

lines.speaker: EMILIA

lines.text: You are a fool; go to.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: O good Iago,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Comfort forswear me! Unkindness may do much;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: But never taint my love. I cannot say “whore:”

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: To do the act that might the addition earn

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Not the world’s mass of vanity could make me.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I pray you, be content; ’tis but his humour:

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Go in, and weep not; all things shall be well.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: I do not find that thou dealest justly with me.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: What in the contrary?

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Every day thou daffest me with some device, Iago; and rather, as

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: it seems to me now, keepest from me all conveniency than suppliest

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: me with the least advantage of hope. I will indeed no longer

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: endure it, nor am I yet persuaded to put up in peace

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: what already I have foolishly suffered.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Will you hear me, Roderigo?

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: ’Faith, I have heard too much, for your words and performances are no kin together.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: You charge me most unjustly.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: With nought but truth. I have wasted myself out of my means.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: The jewels you have had from me to deliver to Desdemona would

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: half have corrupted a votarist: you have told me she hath received

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: them and returned me expectations and comforts of sudden respect and acquaintance,

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: but I find none.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Well; go to; very well.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Very well! go to! I cannot go to, man; nor ’tis not

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: very well: nay, I think it is scurvy, and begin to find

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: myself fobbed in it.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Very well.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: I tell you ’tis not very well. I will make myself known

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: to Desdemona: if she will return me my jewels, I will give

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: over my suit and repent my unlawful solicitation; if not, assure yourself

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: I will seek satisfaction of you.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: You have said now.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Ay, and said nothing but what I protest intendment of doing.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Why, now I see there’s mettle in thee, and even from this

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: instant to build on thee a better opinion than ever before. Give

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: me thy hand, Roderigo: thou hast taken against me a most just

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: exception; but yet, I protest, I have dealt most directly in thy

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: affair.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: It hath not appeared.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I grant indeed it hath not appeared, and your suspicion is not

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: without wit and judgment. But, Roderigo, if thou hast that in thee

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: indeed, which I have greater reason to believe now than ever, I

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: mean purpose, courage and valour, this night show it: if thou the

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: next night following enjoy not Desdemona, take me from this world with

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: treachery and devise engines for my life.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Well, what is it? is it within reason and compass?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Sir, there is especial commission come from Venice to depute Cassio in Othello’s place.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Is that true? why, then Othello and Desdemona return again to Venice.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: O, no; he goes into Mauritania and takes away with him the

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: fair Desdemona, unless his abode be lingered here by some accident: wherein

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: none can be so determinate as the removing of Cassio.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: How do you mean, removing of him?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Why, by making him uncapable of Othello’s place; knocking out his brains.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: And that you would have me to do?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Ay, if you dare do yourself a profit and a right. He

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: sups to-night with a harlotry, and thither will I go to him:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: he knows not yet of his honourable fortune. If you will watch

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: his going thence, which I will fashion to fall out between twelve

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: and one, you may take him at your pleasure: I will be

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: near to second your attempt, and he shall fall between us. Come,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: stand not amazed at it, but go along with me; I will

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: show you such a necessity in his death that you shall think

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: yourself bound to put it on him. It is now high suppertime,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: and the night grows to waste: about it.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: I will hear further reason for this.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And you shall be satisfied.

stage_directions.text: You, you, ay, you! We have done our course; there’s money for your pains: I pray you, turn the key and keep our counsel. Exit.

glosses.definition: by the mass — a Catholic oath
a low-ranking procuress — Othello's insult to Emilia

glosses.phrase: closet lock and key of villainous secrets
glosses.definition: concealed strongbox where secret evils are kept
whore — one of the play's most frequent accusations against Desdemona
body (religious usage: the body as God's vessel)
some utterly damned schemer — Emilia unknowingly names Iago
Desdemona's heartbreaking appeal to her secret destroyer
assassins; men hired to kill me

annotations.title: The brothel scene and the degradation of address
annotations.body:
Othello has Emilia escort him into the bedchamber as if it were a brothel, with Desdemona as the whore and Emilia as the procuress. The structural conceit is horrifying: a husband recasts his wife's private room as a workplace of prostitution, and rehearses his suspicions as if interrogating professionals. The language is sustained — 'simple bawd', 'this office', 'your mystery' — and refuses the consolation of metaphor. Othello means the comparison literally.

The scene reveals how completely Othello's vocabulary has been colonized. He cannot speak to Desdemona without reaching for the lexicon Iago has given him. His final insult — 'a cistern for foul toads / To knot and gender in' — displaces her body into an image of breeding vermin. Language has become his instrument of violence before the physical violence arrives.
sources: Carol Thomas Neely, *Broken Nuptials in Shakespeare's Plays* (1985)

annotations.title: Emilia's unknowing diagnosis
annotations.body:
'Some eternal villain, / Some busy and insinuating rogue, / Some cogging, cozening slave... hath devised this slander.' Emilia names Iago without knowing she names him. The speech is Shakespeare's cruelest dramatic irony: the villain's own wife assembles his character in words, while he stands by noting his next move.

This is the moment the play most clearly shows what Iago has risked and what he gambles. He is protected only by the audience's unwillingness to believe that someone so plainly visible could be invisible to those closest to him. When Emilia does finally see, in 5.2, she sees instantaneously — because the knowledge had always been available. What changed was permission.
sources: Harold Bloom, *Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human* (1998)
Coppélia Kahn, *Man's Estate: Masculine Identity in Shakespeare* (1981)

trials.prompt: Why does Othello treat his conversation with Desdemona as an interview in a brothel, casting Emilia as the procuress?
options: Venetian law required this form of accusation
His vocabulary has been so colonized by Iago that he cannot address his wife except in the language of prostitution — violence of speech preceding physical violence
It is a recognized part of Cypriot marriage rites
Emilia has previously worked in such establishments

trials.prompt: What is the dramatic irony in Emilia's speech about 'some eternal villain, some busy and insinuating rogue'?
options: She is describing her own husband Iago without knowing it — and the audience sees both layers
She is quoting a character from a previous Shakespeare play
She is predicting the arrival of Lodovico from Venice

trials.prompt: What does Iago advise Roderigo to do about Cassio?
options: Ambush and kill him to prevent his return to Desdemona
Challenge him to an open duel
Confess his love for Desdemona directly to him
